34	---
35	
36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Lean Audit Report
100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

63	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
64	
65	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
66	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
67	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
68	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
69	`K(X)`.
70	
71	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
72	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
73	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
74	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
75	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
76	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
77	-/
78	
79	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
80	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
81	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
82	
83	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
84	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
85	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
86	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
87	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
88	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
89	integrality field is needed.
90	
91	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
92	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
93	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
94	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
95	  point : X
96	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
97	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
98	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
99	
100	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
101	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
102	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
103	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
104	
105	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
106	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
107	
108	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
109	
110	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
111	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
112	
113	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
114	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
115	
116	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
117	
118	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
119	
120	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
121	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
122	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
123	
124	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
125	
126	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
127	
128	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
129	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
130	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
131	discrete valuation on `f`.
132	
133	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
134	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
135	
136	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
137	
138	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
139	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
140	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
141	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
142	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
143	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
144	
145	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
146	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
147	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
149	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
150	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
151	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
152	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
153	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
154	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
155	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
156	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
157	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
158	
159	end Scheme.RationalMap

161	/-! ### Regular-in-codimension-one bridge class
162	
163	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
164	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
165	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
166	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`
167	Krull-dim-≤-1 condition required by `Scheme.RationalMap.order`. Blueprint pin
168	("Iter-173+ may introduce a `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` predicate to
169	abbreviate this"; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §2 "Standing hypothesis
170	`(*)` in the Lean encoding"). -/
171	
172	/-- Project-bespoke class encoding Hartshorne's "regular in codimension one"
173	clause of `(*)`: every prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk
174	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes
175	`IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` available so that the
176	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` predicate is well-formed
177	(`AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`). -/
178	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where
179	  /-- The defining content: every prime divisor's stalk is a discrete valuation
180	  ring. (This is the precise content of Hartshorne's `(*)`; the weaker
181	  `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derives via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹
182	  `IsPrincipalIdealRing` ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain via the bridge
183	  instance below.) -/
184	  out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
185	
186	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
187	synthesis can derive `IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for
188	every prime divisor `Y`. -/
189	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk
190	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
191	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
192	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
193	  Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
194	
195	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
196	synthesis can derive `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for every
197	prime divisor `Y`, via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹ `IsPrincipalIdealRing`
198	⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
199	+ `IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one`). -/
200	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
201	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
202	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
203	    Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
204	  haveI : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
205	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
206	  haveI : IsPrincipalIdealRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
207	    IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing
208	  IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one _

210	/-! ### Order-on-curve algebraic identities
211	
212	iter-198 §2 substrate lemmas (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). These are
213	axiom-clean per-prime-divisor algebraic identities on
214	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` — direct consequences of `Ring.ordFrac` being
215	a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom composed with `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
216	(which is additive on nonzero arguments, junk-zero on `0`).
217	
218	The lemmas:
219	
220	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero`: `ord_Y 0 = 0` (junk convention).
221	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero`: `ord_Y (f·g) = ord_Y f +
222	  ord_Y g` when both `f, g ≠ 0`.
223	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_units_inv`: `ord_Y u⁻¹ = -ord_Y u` for a
224	  unit `u : K(X)ˣ`.
225	
226	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` §2 (Hartshorne II §6 valuation
227	identities `v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f)+v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`). -/
228	
229	/-- `ord_Y 0 = 0` by the junk-on-zero convention.
230	
231	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_zero`)
232	and `WithZero.log_zero`. -/
233	@[simp]
234	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero
235	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
236	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
237	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
238	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (0 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
239	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
240	  rw [map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
241	
242	/-- `ord_Y (f · g) = ord_Y f + ord_Y g` when `f, g ≠ 0`.
243	
244	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_mul`)
245	and `WithZero.log_mul` (which requires both factors nonzero). -/
246	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero
247	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
248	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
249	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
250	    {f g : X.functionField} (hf : f ≠ 0) (hg : g ≠ 0) :
251	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (f * g) =
252	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f + Scheme.RationalMap.order Y g := by
253	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
254	  rw [map_mul]
255	  exact WithZero.log_mul ((map_ne_zero _).mpr hf) ((map_ne_zero _).mpr hg)
256	
257	/-- `ord_Y f⁻¹ = -ord_Y f` for any `f : K(X)`.
258	
259	The `f = 0` case is junk-vacuous (`f⁻¹ = 0` in a `GroupWithZero`, both sides

261	
262	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_inv₀`)
263	and `WithZero.log_inv` (which holds for all `WithZero (Multiplicative G)`
264	elements including zero, by the junk-on-zero convention). -/
265	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_inv
266	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
267	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
268	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
269	    (f : X.functionField) :
270	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f⁻¹ = -Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
271	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
272	  rw [map_inv₀, WithZero.log_inv]
273	
274	/-- `ord_Y u⁻¹ = -ord_Y u` for a unit `u : K(X)ˣ`.
275	
276	A specialisation of `order_inv` to the unit form
277	`((u⁻¹ : K(X)ˣ) : K(X)) = (u : K(X))⁻¹`, useful at call sites that thread
278	`Units` (e.g. the `principal_hom : K(X)ˣ →* Multiplicative Div(X)` body). -/
279	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_units_inv
280	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
281	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
282	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
283	    (u : (X.functionField)ˣ) :
284	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ((u⁻¹ : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField) =
285	      -Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (u : X.functionField) := by
286	  rw [show ((u⁻¹ : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField) =
287	        (u : X.functionField)⁻¹ from by simp]
288	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_inv Y _

290	/-- **`ord_Y (-f) = ord_Y f`** for any rational function `f`.
291	
292	The argument: `(-f)^2 = f^2`, so taking `ordFrac` (a monoid-with-zero hom) and
293	then `WithZero.log_pow` gives `2 • ord_Y (-f) = 2 • ord_Y f` in `ℤ`. The free
294	action `(· • ·) : ℕ → ℤ → ℤ` cancels `2 ≠ 0`, giving the equality.

296	iter-199 §2 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). Foundational
297	sign-flip identity for downstream consumers (Hartshorne II.6.10 non-constant

300	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_neg

304	    (f : X.functionField) :

306	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
307	  have h : ((-f) ^ 2) = (f ^ 2) := by ring
308	  have h1 : (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) (-f)) ^ 2 =
309	      (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f) ^ 2 := by

312	      ((Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f) ^ 2).log := by rw [h1]
313	  rw [WithZero.log_pow, WithZero.log_pow] at h2
314	  exact (smul_right_injective ℤ (by norm_num : (2 : ℕ) ≠ 0)) h2

328	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
329	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)

331	    {f : X.functionField} (hf : f ≠ 0) (n : ℕ) :
332	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (f ^ n) = n * Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by

336	    rw [pow_succ, Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero Y (pow_ne_zero k hf) hf, ih]

340	/-- **Hartshorne II.6.1**: for a nonzero rational function `f` on a Noetherian
341	integral scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, the order function `Y ↦ ord_Y(f)` is
342	nonzero at only finitely many prime divisors `Y`. This is the well-definedness
343	side condition for `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal`.
344	
345	iter-177 status: this packages Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, which Mathlib does not
346	ship. The body is a Mathlib-upstream-pending gap (Stacks tag `02RV` — for a
347	nonzero element `f ∈ K(X)^×` of a Noetherian integral scheme, only finitely
348	many height-one primes can divide either numerator or denominator); the proof
349	factors through `IsLocallyNoetherian X` + the principal-ideal generation of
350	height-1 primes + the finite irreducible-component decomposition of
351	`V(f₀) ∪ V(f∞)`. The chapter pins this as a separate sub-build deferral
352	(`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5).
353	
354	The statement is generic in `f` (no `f ≠ 0` hypothesis is threaded): on
355	`f = 0` the function `Y ↦ ord_Y(0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` has empty support,
356	which is finite, so the conclusion holds vacuously. -/
357	private theorem rationalMap_order_finite_support {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
358	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
359	    (f : X.functionField) :
360	    (Function.support (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor =>
361	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)).Finite := by
362	  -- **iter-192 case split + f = 0 branch closed axiom-clean.**
363	  --
364	  -- Case 1 (f = 0): the order function evaluates to
365	  -- `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) = WithZero.log 0 = 0` at every
366	  -- prime divisor, so the support is empty (finite vacuously).
367	  -- iter-198 cleanup: use `Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero` for clarity.
368	  by_cases hf : f = 0
369	  · subst hf
370	    convert Set.finite_empty
371	    ext Y
372	    simp only [Function.mem_support, ne_eq, Set.mem_empty_iff_false, iff_false,
373	      Decidable.not_not, Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero]

381	  --       Noetherian integral domain (by `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]` +
382	  --       `[IsIntegral X]`); `K(X) = Frac(R)`; write `f = a/b`.

388	  --       this to be finite, `X \ U` must be a Noetherian topological space —
389	  --       which holds iff `X` is itself globally Noetherian (= locally
390	  --       Noetherian + quasi-compact via `AlgebraicGeometry.isNoetherian_iff`).
391	  --
392	  -- The current signature has only `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`; without

394	  -- bounded — a counter-example is any non-quasi-compact integral locally

419	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}

421	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
422	
423	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
424	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
425	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
426	
427	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
428	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
429	
430	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
431	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
432	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
433	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
434	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
435	
436	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
437	
438	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
439	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
440	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
441	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
442	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
443	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
444	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
445	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
446	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
447	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
448	hypothesised regime. -/
449	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
450	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
451	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
452	
453	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
454	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
455	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
456	multiplicity one. -/
457	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
458	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
459	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
460	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
461	
462	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
463	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
464	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
465	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
466	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
467	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
468	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
469	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
470	
471	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
472	
473	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
474	
475	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
476	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
477	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
478	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
479	
480	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
481	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
482	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
483	
484	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
485	
486	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
487	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
488	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
489	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
490	
491	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
492	
493	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
494	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
495	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
496	
497	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
498	
499	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
500	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
501	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
502	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
503	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
504	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
505	
506	@[simp]
507	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
508	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
509	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
510	
511	/-- **Degree of the zero divisor is zero.** A direct consequence of
512	`degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`. -/
513	@[simp]
514	lemma degree_zero : degree (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
515	  rw [← degree_hom_apply]; exact map_zero _
516	
517	/-- **Degree is additive.** A direct consequence of `degree_hom` being an
518	`AddMonoidHom`. -/
519	lemma degree_add (D₁ D₂ : X.WeilDivisor) :
520	    degree (D₁ + D₂) = degree D₁ + degree D₂ := by
521	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
522	  exact map_add _ _ _
523	
524	/-- **Degree of the negation.** `deg(-D) = -deg D`. A direct consequence of
525	`degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`.
526	
527	iter-198 §4 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). -/
528	@[simp]
529	lemma degree_neg (D : X.WeilDivisor) :
530	    degree (-D) = -degree D := by
531	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
532	  exact map_neg _ _
533	
534	/-- **Degree is subtractive.** `deg(D₁ - D₂) = deg D₁ - deg D₂`. A direct
535	consequence of `degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`.
536	
537	iter-198 §4 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). -/
538	lemma degree_sub (D₁ D₂ : X.WeilDivisor) :
539	    degree (D₁ - D₂) = degree D₁ - degree D₂ := by
540	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
541	  exact map_sub _ _ _
542	
543	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
544	
545	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
546	
547	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
548	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
549	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
550	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
551	
552	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
553	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
554	
555	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
556	following definition, p. 131).
557	
558	iter-177 body: the construction uses `Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` with the
559	finite-support witness `rationalMap_order_finite_support`. The latter is a
560	private theorem packaging Hartshorne 6.1; its body is a Mathlib-pending gap
561	(see chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5 sub-build note) and is left as
562	a `sorry` for an iter-178+ Mathlib-upstream PR. -/
563	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
564	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField)
565	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
566	  Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
567	    (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
568	    (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)
569	
570	/-- **The coefficient of `principal f hf` at a prime divisor `Y` is the order of
571	`f` along `Y`.** This is the basic structural unfolding of the
572	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` packaging in `principal`; one-line via
573	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe` from `Mathlib.Data.Finsupp.Defs`.
574	
575	iter-193 substrate helper for the Lane I body close
576	(`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`). -/
577	lemma principal_apply [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
578	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
579	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) :
580	    (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y =
581	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
582	  change (Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
583	      (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
584	      (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)) Y = _
585	  rw [Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe]
586	
587	/-- **`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y 1 = 0`** — the order of the constant
588	function `1` is `0` at every prime divisor. Direct from
589	`map_one` of `Ring.ordFrac` + `WithZero.log_one`.
590	
591	iter-193 substrate helper for further structural results
592	(`principal_one_eq_zero` and similar). -/
593	@[simp]
594	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_one
595	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
596	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
597	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
598	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (1 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
599	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
600	  rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
601	
602	/-- **The principal divisor of the constant function `1` is the zero
603	divisor.** A direct consequence of `Scheme.RationalMap.order_one`. -/
604	@[simp]
605	lemma principal_one [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
606	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
607	    principal (1 : X.functionField) one_ne_zero = 0 := by
608	  change (_ : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
609	  apply Finsupp.ext
610	  intro Y
611	  rw [principal_apply]
612	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_one Y
613	
614	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
615	
616	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
617	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
618	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
619	
620	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
621	
622	iter-177 body: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities
623	`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`. The per-`Y` identities live in
624	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` via `Ring.ordFrac` (a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero
625	hom) and `WithZero.log_mul` / `WithZero.log_one`. -/
626	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
627	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
628	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor where
629	  toFun u :=
630	    Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal (↑u : X.functionField) (Units.ne_zero u))
631	  map_one' := by
632	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑1 _) = 1.
633	    -- Use `← ofAdd_zero` to rewrite RHS as `Multiplicative.ofAdd 0`,
634	    -- then `congr 1` reduces to `principal ↑1 _ = 0` (Finsupp equality),
635	    -- and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate identity, which
636	    -- closes by unfolding `order` to `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 1) = 0`
637	    -- via `map_one` and `WithZero.log_one`.
638	    rw [← ofAdd_zero]
639	    congr 1
640	    apply Finsupp.ext
641	    intro Y
642	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y
643	        (((1 : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField)) = 0
644	    rw [Units.val_one]
645	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
646	    rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
647	  map_mul' u v := by
648	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑(u*v) _) =
649	    --       Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑u _) * Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑v _).
650	    -- Use `← ofAdd_add` to rewrite RHS, then `congr 1` reduces to
651	    -- `principal ↑(u*v) _ = principal ↑u _ + principal ↑v _` (Finsupp
652	    -- equality), and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate
653	    -- identity `order Y (uv) = order Y u + order Y v`, which closes by
654	    -- `Ring.ordFrac (uv) = Ring.ordFrac u * Ring.ordFrac v` (`map_mul`)
655	    -- and `WithZero.log_mul` (the nonzero hypotheses come from
656	    -- `Units.ne_zero` and `map_ne_zero`).
657	    rw [← ofAdd_add]
658	    congr 1
659	    apply Finsupp.ext
660	    intro Y
661	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ((↑(u * v) : X.functionField))
662	      = Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑u : X.functionField)
663	      + Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑v : X.functionField)
664	    rw [Units.val_mul]
665	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
666	    rw [map_mul]
667	    exact WithZero.log_mul
668	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero u))
669	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero v))
670	
671	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
672	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
673	
674	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
675	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
676	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
677	
678	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
679	
680	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
681	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
682	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
683	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
684	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
685	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
686	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
687	iters of `RR.1`. -/
688	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
689	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
690	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
691	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
692	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
693	    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
694	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 := by
695	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10 (iter-178 partial). Case-split on whether every
696	  -- prime-divisor order of `f` vanishes. On a complete nonsingular curve,
697	  -- this case split exactly recovers Hartshorne's constant-vs-non-constant
698	  -- split: `(∀ Y, ord_Y f = 0)` ⟺ `f ∈ k̄ \ {0}` (a nowhere-vanishing
699	  -- rational function on a complete curve is constant; that direction is
700	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10's "if `f ∈ k̄` then `(f) = 0`").
701	  by_cases hconst : ∀ Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
702	  · -- Constant branch: every order vanishes ⟹ `principal f hf = 0` ⟹
703	    -- `degree (principal f hf) = degree 0 = 0`.
704	    have hprincipal_zero : principal f hf = 0 := by
705	      apply Finsupp.ext
706	      intro Y
707	      change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
708	      exact hconst Y
709	    rw [hprincipal_zero]
710	    unfold degree
711	    exact Finsupp.sum_zero_index
712	  · -- Non-constant branch (Hartshorne II.6.10 `f ∉ k̄`): the inclusion
713	    -- `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` defines a finite morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` via the
714	    -- function-field-determines-curve correspondence (Hartshorne I.6.12),
715	    -- and `principal f hf = φ^*([0] - [∞])`. Pullback along a finite
716	    -- morphism of curves multiplies degrees by `deg(φ)` (Hartshorne
717	    -- II.6.9), so `deg(principal f hf) = deg(φ) · deg([0] - [∞]) =
718	    -- deg(φ) · 0 = 0`.
719	    -- This branch is gated on (i) the `φ : C → ℙ¹` construction (Lane 5
720	    -- `RationalCurveIso.lean:morphismToP1OfGlobalSections`, iter-178+),
721	    -- and (ii) the degree-multiplicativity-under-finite-pullback bridge
722	    -- (Hartshorne II.6.9, Mathlib gap).
723	    sorry
724	
725	/-! ## §6. Positive part of a Weil divisor
726	
727	Iter-190 plan-phase Lane I Pin 2 corrective substrate. The carrier
728	`X.WeilDivisor = X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ` is a finitely supported integer-valued
729	function on prime divisors; the *positive part* `(D)_0` is obtained by
730	replacing each coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)`. Equivalently this is
731	the lattice join `D ⊔ 0` in the pointwise lattice structure (the
732	`semilatticeSup` instance on `Finsupp` is noncomputable). The complementary
733	*negative part* `(D)_∞ := (-D)_0` then gives the canonical decomposition
734	`D = (D)_0 - (D)_∞` into a difference of effective divisors.
735	
736	Blueprint reference: `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart` /
737	`lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` of
738	`RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §6 (iter-190 plan-phase additions).
739	-/
740	
741	/-- **Positive part of a Weil divisor**.
742	
743	`positivePart D` is the divisor obtained from `D` by replacing each
744	coefficient `n_Y` with `max(n_Y, 0)` (equivalently `D ⊔ 0` in the
745	pointwise lattice structure on `Finsupp`). For `D = div(f)` on a smooth
746	proper curve `C`, this recovers the divisor-of-zeros `(f)_0` — the
747	formal sum of the zeros of `f` with their multiplicities.
748	
749	The complementary `negativePart D := positivePart (-D)` then gives
750	`D = positivePart D - negativePart D` as a difference of effective
751	divisors.
752	
753	Implementation: `Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)`, the
754	generic Mathlib packaging that maps a finsupp's coefficients through a
755	zero-preserving function. (The `D ⊔ 0` lattice form is equivalent but
756	requires the noncomputable `Finsupp.semilatticeSup` synthesis on
757	`PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ`; the explicit `mapRange` form is more transparent
758	to typeclass synthesis.)
759	
760	Blueprint reference: `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart` (project-bespoke;
761	chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §6, Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing). -/
762	noncomputable def positivePart (D : X.WeilDivisor) : X.WeilDivisor :=
763	  Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp) D
764	
765	/-- The positive part of the zero divisor is zero. -/
766	@[simp]
767	lemma positivePart_zero : positivePart (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
768	  change Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)
769	    (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
770	  exact Finsupp.mapRange_zero
771	
772	/-- **Degree of positive part as a sum of capped coefficients.** A purely
773	symbolic Mathlib manipulation: unfolding `positivePart`
774	(= `Finsupp.mapRange (max · 0)`) and `degree` (= `Finsupp.sum (fun _ n => n)`)
775	identifies `degree (positivePart D)` with the sum of `max (D Y) 0` over
776	the support of `D`.
777	
778	This is the iter-192 structural-reduction helper consumed by
779	`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` below. The proof is one line
780	via `Finsupp.sum_mapRange_index` from
781	`Mathlib.Algebra.BigOperators.Finsupp.Basic`. -/
782	lemma degree_positivePart_eq_sum_max (D : X.WeilDivisor) :
783	    degree (positivePart D) = D.sum (fun _ n => max n 0) := by
784	  unfold positivePart degree
785	  exact Finsupp.sum_mapRange_index (h := fun _ b => b) (by intro _; rfl)
786	
787	/-- **Positive part of a `Finsupp.single`.** Pointwise extraction of the
788	mapRange definition of `positivePart`: for a one-point-supported Weil divisor
789	`Finsupp.single Y n`, the positive part is `Finsupp.single Y (max n 0)`.
790	A direct consequence of `Finsupp.mapRange_single` (with `max 0 0 = 0` as
791	the zero-preservation witness).
792	
793	iter-193 substrate helper paving the way for the Lane I body close. -/
794	@[simp]
795	lemma positivePart_single (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ) :
796	    positivePart (Finsupp.single Y n : X.WeilDivisor) =
797	      Finsupp.single Y (max n 0) := by
798	  change Finsupp.mapRange (fun n : ℤ => n ⊔ 0) (by simp)
799	      (Finsupp.single Y n) = _
800	  rw [Finsupp.mapRange_single]
801	
802	/-- **Degree of a `Finsupp.single` Weil divisor.** The degree of the
803	one-point-supported Weil divisor `Finsupp.single Y n` is `n`. A direct
804	consequence of `Finsupp.sum_single_index`.
805	
806	iter-193 substrate helper for `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. -/
807	@[simp]
808	lemma degree_single (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (n : ℤ) :
809	    degree (Finsupp.single Y n : X.WeilDivisor) = n := by
810	  unfold degree
811	  change (Finsupp.single Y n : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n) = n
812	  exact Finsupp.sum_single_index rfl
813	
814	/-- **Sum-over-prime-divisors lower bound via a single contributing point.**
815	If `f` has order `1` at some prime divisor `Y₀`, then the degree of the positive
816	part of `principal f hf` is at least `1`.
817	
818	iter-193 substrate helper consumed by `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`:
819	it formalises Step 2 of the Hartshorne II.6.9 recipe (`hlp` produces
820	the local-parameter prime divisor `Y₀` with coefficient `1`). -/
821	lemma one_le_degree_positivePart_principal_of_order_one
822	    [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
823	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
824	    (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) (Y₀ : X.PrimeDivisor)
825	    (h₀ : Scheme.RationalMap.order Y₀ f = 1) :
826	    1 ≤ degree (positivePart (principal f hf)) := by
827	  classical
828	  rw [degree_positivePart_eq_sum_max]
829	  -- The sum is over a finset; isolate the Y₀ contribution.
830	  have hY₀_supp :
831	      Y₀ ∈ (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support := by
832	    rw [Finsupp.mem_support_iff]
833	    rw [principal_apply]
834	    rw [h₀]
835	    exact one_ne_zero
836	  -- For the sum to be ≥ 1, isolate the Y₀ term (= 1) and the rest (≥ 0).
837	  have h_split :
838	      (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).sum
839	          (fun _ n => max n 0) =
840	        max ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y₀) 0 +
841	          ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support.erase Y₀).sum
842	            (fun Y => max
843	              ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y) 0) := by
844	    rw [show (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).sum
845	            (fun _ n => max n 0) =
846	          (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support.sum
847	            (fun Y => max
848	              ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y) 0)
849	        from rfl]
850	    rw [Finset.sum_erase_eq_sub hY₀_supp]
851	    ring
852	  rw [h_split]
853	  -- The Y₀ term equals max 1 0 = 1.
854	  have hY₀_val :
855	      (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y₀ = 1 := by
856	    rw [principal_apply]; exact h₀
857	  rw [hY₀_val]
858	  -- The remainder is a sum of non-negative integers, hence ≥ 0.
859	  have h_nonneg :
860	      0 ≤ ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf).support.erase Y₀).sum
861	        (fun Y => max
862	          ((show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y) 0) :=
863	    Finset.sum_nonneg (fun Y _ => le_max_right _ _)
864	  -- Conclude: 1 = max 1 0 ≤ max 1 0 + 0 ≤ LHS + remainder.
865	  have : (max (1 : ℤ) 0) = 1 := by norm_num
866	  rw [this]
867	  linarith
868	
869	/-- **Generic Finsupp identity**: the sum of clipped non-negative parts equals
870	the sum of the coefficients over the positive-coefficient sub-support.
871	
872	For a finsupp `D : α →₀ ℤ`, `D.sum (max · 0)` agrees with the unclipped sum
873	`∑ D` restricted to the finset `{a ∈ supp D | 0 < D a}`. Negative-coefficient
874	points contribute `0` to the max and drop out; positive-coefficient points
875	contribute `D a` to both sides; the (vacuously zero) `D a = 0` case is excluded
876	by the `supp` filter on both sides.
877	
878	iter-195 Lane I substrate helper for `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`:
879	it formalises the "the positive part of `D` is supported on the positive-coefficient
880	points" identity, which is Step 2.5 of the Hartshorne II.6.9 recipe (between the
881	`degree_positivePart_eq_sum_max` unfolding and the ramification-inertia chase). -/
882	lemma _root_.Finsupp.sum_max_zero_eq_sum_filter_pos {α : Type*}
883	    (D : α →₀ ℤ) :
884	    D.sum (fun _ n => max n 0) =
885	      ∑ a ∈ D.support.filter (fun a => 0 < D a), D a := by
886	  classical
887	  rw [Finsupp.sum, Finset.sum_filter]
888	  apply Finset.sum_congr rfl
889	  intro a _
890	  by_cases hpos : 0 < D a
891	  · simp [hpos]
892	    omega
893	  · simp [hpos]
894	    omega

896	/-! ### Iter-194 typed-sorry instance scaffolding for `ProjectiveLineBar kbar`
897	
898	The `degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` theorem below specialises
899	`K = (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField` and the uniformiser
900	witness `hLPUnif` quantifies over `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.PrimeDivisor`
901	with `Scheme.RationalMap.order Y₀ t`, which requires the underlying
902	scheme to support the order machinery. The required typeclasses are
903	`IsIntegral`, `IsLocallyNoetherian`, and `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`
904	on `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`. These are mathematically true
905	(ProjectiveLineBar is `Proj` of `k̄[X₀, X₁]`, finite type over the field
906	`k̄`, smooth of dimension 1 over `k̄`, hence locally Noetherian, integral,
907	and regular at every point), but Mathlib does not currently ship them as
908	free instances. We declare them as named typed-sorry instances scheduled
909	for iter-194+ closure.
910	-/
911	
912	/-- **Iter-194 axiom-clean closure**: `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` is locally
913	Noetherian. Derivation chain: `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom` is proper (existing
914	`projectiveLineBar_isProper`), hence locally of finite type
915	(`IsProper.toLocallyOfFiniteType`); the base `Spec (.of kbar)` is locally

917	over a locally Noetherian base has locally Noetherian source
918	(`LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian`). -/
919	instance instIsLocallyNoetherianProjectiveLineBar (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] :
920	    IsLocallyNoetherian (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left := by
921	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec (.of kbar)) := inferInstance
922	  haveI : LocallyOfFiniteType (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom :=
923	    IsProper.toLocallyOfFiniteType
924	  exact LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom
925	
926	/-- **Typed-sorry theorem** (iter-196+ demoted from `instance`):
927	`(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` is regular in codimension one (Hartshorne's
928	`(*)`). Derivation: smoothness ⟹ regular ⟹ DVR stalks at codim-1
929	points; the bridge from `SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1` to
930	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` is iter-194+ work.
931	**Demoted from `instance` per lean-auditor iter-196 must-fix**: the silent
932	propagation of `sorryAx` through `IsRegularInCodimensionOne`-typeclass
933	synthesis is a soundness exposure. Callers thread via
934	`haveI := isRegularInCodimOneProjectiveLineBar kbar`. -/
935	theorem isRegularInCodimOneProjectiveLineBar (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
936	    [IsIntegral (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left] :
937	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left := by
938	  -- **Iter-195 structural advance.** Expose the per-prime-divisor obligation.
939	  -- The body's recipe is the standard "smooth-of-dim-1 ⟹ DVR stalks at
940	  -- every codim-1 point" chain, which decomposes as:
941	  --   (i)  `SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom`
942	  --        (gated on BareScheme `projectiveLineBar_smoothOfRelDim` sorry);
943	  --   (ii) smooth scheme ⟹ each stalk is a regular local ring (Mathlib
944	  --        gap: no direct `AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.isRegularLocalRing_stalk`
945	  --        bridge ships in `b80f227`; iter-196+ Lane I directive Step A);
946	  --   (iii) `Y.coheight = 1` ⟹ stalk Krull dim = 1 (Mathlib gap, Stacks
947	  --        02IZ / 005X — topological coheight ↔ algebraic Krull dim);
948	  --   (iv) regular local ring of Krull dim 1 ⟹ DVR (Mathlib available
949	  --        via `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace` +
950	  --        `IsLocalRing.finrank_CotangentSpace_eq_one_iff`).
951	  refine ⟨fun Y => ?_⟩
952	  -- Per-prime-divisor obligation:
953	  -- `IsDiscreteValuationRing ((ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.presheaf.stalk Y.point)`
954	  -- with `Y.coheight : Order.coheight Y.point = 1` in scope.
955	  --
956	  -- **Iter-196 Lane I Route 2 structural advance** (PID-transfer via the
957	  -- 2-chart affine cover). The closure decomposes as:
958	  --
959	  --   (A) Pick the chart `i := 𝒰.idx Y.point` containing `Y.point` (where
960	  --       `𝒰 = (projectiveLineBarAffineCover kbar).openCover`), with the
961	  --       chart-side witness `y : Spec(Away 𝒜 (X i))` such that
962	  --       `(𝒰.f i).base y = Y.point`.
963	  --
964	  --   (B) Open-immersion stalk transfer: `(𝒰.f i)` is an open immersion
965	  --       (the chart is an open immersion into `ProjectiveLineBar`), so the
966	  --       induced map `(𝒰.f i).stalkMap y : stalk(Proj, Y.point) ⟶
967	  --       stalk(chart, y)` is an iso (`IsOpenImmersion.iff_isIso_stalkMap`).
968	  --
969	  --   (C) The chart's stalk at `y` identifies with `Localization.AtPrime
970	  --       y.asIdeal` via `Spec.stalkIso`. The chart's ring `Away 𝒜 (X i)`
971	  --       is isomorphic to `MvPolynomial Unit kbar` (project-local
972	  --       `homogeneousLocalizationAwayIso`); composing with `pUnitAlgEquiv`
973	  --       gives a ring iso to `Polynomial kbar`, a PID (Mathlib's
974	  --       `Polynomial.instEuclideanDomain` + `EuclideanDomain.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
975	  --       + `IsPrincipalIdealRing.isDedekindDomain`).
976	  --
977	  --   (D) Conclude: in a Dedekind domain, localization at a non-zero prime
978	  --       ideal is a DVR via Mathlib's
979	  --       `IsLocalization.AtPrime.isDiscreteValuationRing_of_dedekind_domain`.
980	  --
981	  -- The residual sub-claim — "the prime corresponding to `Y.point` in the
982	  -- chart is non-zero (equivalently: maximal, equivalently: `Y.point` is a
983	  -- closed point of `ProjectiveLineBar`)" — uses the coheight-1 hypothesis
984	  -- `Y.coheight : Order.coheight Y.point = 1`. The bridge from topological
985	  -- coheight to "prime is non-zero in the local affine chart" is a
986	  -- Mathlib-pending substrate (Stacks 02IZ / 005X; ``coheight on the
987	  -- specialisation preorder of a 1-dim integral scheme ⟹ point is
988	  -- closed''). This iter-196 advance lays the chart-pick + stalk-transfer
989	  -- skeleton; the final per-chart maximality bridge is the residual sorry.
990	  --
991	  -- **Step (A)**: Chart selection via the affine open cover.
992	  let 𝒰 := (projectiveLineBarAffineCover kbar).openCover
993	  let i := 𝒰.idx Y.point
994	  obtain ⟨y, hy⟩ := 𝒰.covers Y.point
995	  -- Now `y : 𝒰.X i = Spec(Away 𝒜 (X i))` with `(𝒰.f i).base y = Y.point`.
996	  --
997	  -- **Step (B)**: Open-immersion stalk transfer. The chart morphism is an
998	  -- open immersion, so its stalk map is an iso.
999	  have hopen : IsOpenImmersion (𝒰.f i) := inferInstance
1000	  haveI hstalkIso : IsIso ((𝒰.f i).stalkMap y) :=
1001	    (IsOpenImmersion.iff_isIso_stalkMap.mp hopen).2 y
1002	  -- Step (B′): Transport `IsDiscreteValuationRing` from the chart stalk to
1003	  -- the Proj stalk along the inverse of the (iso) stalkMap.
1004	  -- The stalk map source is `Proj.presheaf.stalk ((𝒰.f i).base y)`; rewrite
1005	  -- to `Proj.presheaf.stalk Y.point` using `hy`.
1006	  have hbase : (𝒰.f i).base y = Y.point := hy
1007	  rw [← hbase]
1008	  -- New goal: `IsDiscreteValuationRing (Proj.presheaf.stalk ((𝒰.f i).base y))`.
1009	  -- Set up the stalk iso (now usable since `hstalkIso : IsIso ((𝒰.f i).stalkMap y)`
1010	  -- is in scope):
1011	  have hiso : (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.presheaf.stalk ((𝒰.f i).base y) ≅
1012	      (𝒰.X i).presheaf.stalk y := @asIso _ _ _ _ _ hstalkIso
1013	  -- Domain on chart stalk: transport from `IsDomain (Proj stalk)` (which holds
1014	  -- since `(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` is integral) via the iso `hiso`.
1015	  haveI hDomProj : IsDomain ((ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.presheaf.stalk
1016	      ((𝒰.f i).base y)) := inferInstance
1017	  haveI hDomChart : IsDomain ((𝒰.X i).presheaf.stalk y) :=
1018	    MulEquiv.isDomain _ hiso.symm.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv.toMulEquiv
1019	  -- Suffices: the chart stalk at `y` is a DVR; transport along stalkMap iso.
1020	  suffices hchart : IsDiscreteValuationRing ((𝒰.X i).presheaf.stalk y) by
1021	    -- Apply RingEquiv-transport: chart ≃+* Proj.
1022	    exact IsDiscreteValuationRing.RingEquivClass.isDiscreteValuationRing
1023	      (hiso.symm.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv)
1024	  -- New goal: `IsDiscreteValuationRing ((𝒰.X i).presheaf.stalk y)`.
1025	  --
1026	  -- **Step (C)**: Identify chart stalk with `Localization.AtPrime y.asIdeal`
1027	  -- via `Spec.stalkIso`. The chart `(𝒰.X i)` is by construction
1028	  -- `Spec ((projectiveLineBarAffineCover kbar).X i) = Spec(Away 𝒜 (X i))`;
1029	  -- the stalk at the point `y : Spec(Away 𝒜 (X i))` is `Localization.AtPrime
1030	  -- y.asIdeal` (Mathlib `Spec.stalkIso`).
1031	  have hspecstalk :
1032	      (𝒰.X i).presheaf.stalk y ≅
1033	      CommRingCat.of (Localization.AtPrime y.asIdeal) :=
1034	    Spec.stalkIso ((projectiveLineBarAffineCover kbar).X i) y
1035	  -- **Step (D)**: Establish PID / Dedekind structure on the chart ring
1036	  -- `Away (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) (X i)` via the iso to
1037	  -- `MvPolynomial Unit kbar ≃ Polynomial kbar` (a Euclidean domain → PID →
1038	  -- Dedekind).
1039	  --
1040	  -- The `(![X 0, X 1]) i` form appearing in the cover construction reduces
1041	  -- definitionally to `MvPolynomial.X i`; we use the explicit `heq` to bridge.
1042	  -- (`i` has type `𝒰.I₀ = Fin 2` but Lean needs a manual cast for `fin_cases`;
1043	  -- we prove the universal statement and instantiate.)
1044	  have heq : (![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
1045	      Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) i = MvPolynomial.X i := by
1046	    have h : ∀ (j : Fin 2), (![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
1047	        Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) j = MvPolynomial.X j := by
1048	      intro j; fin_cases j <;> rfl
1049	    exact h i
1050	  haveI hAwayIsDomain : IsDomain
1051	      (HomogeneousLocalization.Away (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar)
1052	        ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
1053	            Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) i)) := by

8	/-!
9	# Auslander–Buchsbaum formula (A.4.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.4.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
14	⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
15	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective surface)
16	consumes.
17	
18	Per STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
19	startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the Albanese
20	geometry and lives entirely under `RingTheory.*`. Mathlib at the project's
21	pinned commit (`b80f227`) exposes `IsRegularLocalRing` and the categorical
22	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on an abelian category (specialised to
23	`ModuleCat R`), as well as the regular-sequence definition
24	`RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`. The depth function `Module.depth`, the
25	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula, and the Cohen–Macaulay predicate are *not* in
26	Mathlib at the pinned commit — they are the new content scaffolded here.
27	
28	## Status (iter-178 update — projectiveDimension closed)
29	
30	This file was originally landed iter-175 (Lane F file-skeleton) with each
31	blueprint-pinned declaration carrying the *intended* substantive type
32	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
33	`blueprint/src/chapters/Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum.tex`). The bodies were
34	scheduled for iter-176+ work; the chapter is independent of the rest of
35	Route A's geometric infrastructure, making it a good parallel-work
36	candidate.
37	
38	iter-178 closed `Module.projectiveDimension` kernel-clean as a one-liner
39	re-export of the categorical `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension`. The
40	remaining five typed `sorry` bodies (`depth`, `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`,
41	`depth_of_short_exact`, `auslander_buchsbaum_formula`,
42	`CohenMacaulay.of_regular`) are substantive multi-iter content and stay
43	gated on dedicated body lanes.
44	
45	The 7 pinned declarations are:
46	
47	1. `RingTheory.Module.depth` (noncomputable def, ~6 LOC) — the `I`-depth of a
48	   finite `R`-module as the supremum of lengths of `M`-regular sequences in
49	   `I`, valued in `ℕ∞`.
50	2. `RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — for
51	   `(R, 𝔪)` Noetherian local and `M ≠ 0` finite, `depth(M)` equals the
52	   smallest `i` with `Ext^i_R(κ, M) ≠ 0`. Encoded via the depth-bound iff
53	   `Ext` vanishes below.
54	3. `Module.projectiveDimension` (noncomputable def, ~3 LOC) — an
55	   `R`-module-side wrapper for the categorical
56	   `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R M)`. The categorical
57	   version exists in Mathlib `b80f227`; this is the re-export pinned by the
58	   blueprint.
59	4. `RingTheory.depth_of_short_exact` (theorem, ~12 LOC) — the
60	   depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequalities (Stacks 00LE).
61	5. `RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula` (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the formula
62	   `pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)` for a nonzero finite `R`-module of finite
63	   projective dimension over a Noetherian local ring.
64	6. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay` (class, ~3 LOC) — `IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing R`
65	   encoded as `depth(R) = krullDim R`.
66	7. `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular` (theorem, ~6 LOC) — a regular local
67	   ring is Cohen–Macaulay (the consumer-facing input for A.4.a).
68	
69	## Note on type expressivity
70	
71	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
72	declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
73	
74	- `depth` returns `ℕ∞` and is defined by the regular-sequence supremum.
75	- `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` is encoded as the depth-bound `↔` `Ext`
76	  vanishing characterisation (the smallest non-vanishing `Ext` index = depth).
77	- `projectiveDimension` re-exports the categorical
78	  `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on `ModuleCat.of R M`.
79	- `depth_of_short_exact` packages the three Stacks 00LE inequalities into a
80	  conjunction.
81	- `auslander_buchsbaum_formula` is the numeric equation
82	  `pd + depth = depth(R)`.
83	- `CohenMacaulay` is the equation `depth(R) = ringKrullDim R`.
84	- `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` is an `IsRegularLocalRing → IsCohenMacaulayLocalRing`
85	  implication, the consumer-facing statement.
86	
87	Unfolding any declaration exposes the named substantive content (a regular-
88	sequence supremum, an `Ext`-vanishing characterisation, the categorical
89	projective dimension, …); no `Iso.refl _` / `Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` /
